title: Claude Just Dropped ULTRA CODE. (Everything You Need to Know in 5 min).
summary: Claude released Ultra Code, a feature that coordinates up to 100 parallel workers to decompose and execute tasks concurrently, then verify results. The feature combines extended thinking with dynamic workflows to enable team-like code generation and task execution at scale.
writeup: This video walks through Claude Code's Ultra Code setting, explaining how it bundles two distinct capabilities: an extra-high thinking level (one tier below Max) applied to every step, and dynamic workflows that spin up a team of parallel agents, each tackling a different sub-task, with a separate agent then checking the combined output. The through-line is distinguishing Ultra Code from the Max effort level, showing via a food-truck research demo how concurrent agents compress multi-tab research into seconds, then quantifying the cost tradeoff the documentation itself calls out.
key_points: Ultra Code is not a higher effort tier than Max; it is a separate mode that combines extra-high thinking on every step with dynamic multi-agent workflows, activated once per session via the /effort menu.

Dynamic workflows follow a three-phase pattern: Claude first forms a plan, then spawns multiple agents each pursuing a different sub-task concurrently (up to 100 agents depending on hardware), then routes the combined result to a dedicated checking agent before returning output.

Max effort maximizes depth of thought in a single chain of reasoning, while Ultra Code trades some thinking depth (extra-high rather than Max) for breadth via parallelism; choosing between them depends on whether the bottleneck is reasoning depth or task decomposition.

In the food-truck research demo, a rising lightning-bolt counter in the UI showed agents simultaneously chasing permits, equipment costs, and supplier sourcing, producing a fact-checked starter guide in far less time than sequential single-thread execution.

Ultra Code is explicitly the most expensive way to run Claude Code by a significant margin, a fact the official documentation calls out directly; running /usage after a session breaks down the token and credit cost of a run.
